data.title: Lithium Miners News For The Month Of December 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the December 2025 edition of the lithium miner news.

The past month saw lithium prices significantly higher again.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported lithium spodumene concentrate Index (Li2O 5.5%-6.2%, excluding tax/insurance/freight) spot price of USD 1,378, as of December 22, 2025, significantly higher the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price was significantly higher last month, now at CNY 99,000. The CIF Asia lithium carbonate price is ~US$11.38/kg.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 99,000 (~USD 14,060) (source)

Lithium demand versus supply outlook

BloombergNEF forecasts lithium deficits may begin in 2025 (as of Oct. 2024) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

Albemarle is forecasting lithium deficits more likely from ~2027/28 (as of mid 2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid 2025)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

Lithium market and battery news

In November Iola Hughes posted on X:

Shipments of #BESS cells are surging in 2025, set to exceed 550 GWh this year, a 76% y-o-y increase. By comparison, installations are set to be closer to 300 GWh. So why does these differ - time lags between a #battery being made and a project coming online vary widely.

On November 24 Seeking Alpha reported:

U.S. Export-Import Bank plans to spend $100B for critical minerals, energy dominance - FT. The U.S. Export-Import Bank will spend $100 billion to secure U.S. supply chains for critical minerals, nuclear energy and liquefied natural gas...The chair said the bank authorized $8.7 billion in new transactions in the 12 months to the end of September...Nuclear energy will be a focus under the bank’s new leadership.

On November 25 MySteel reported:

Tianqi Lithium expects global lithium demand to reach 2 million tonnes of LCE by 2026...Global lithium demand is projected to reach 2 million tonnes of lithium carbonate equivalent in 2026, essentially achieving a supply-demand balance.

On December 10 Bloomberg reported:

Like clockwork, battery prices keep ticking lower. Lithium-ion battery prices dropped again in 2025, with average prices coming down 8% to $108 per kilowatt-hour, according to BloombergNEF’s annual price survey...China’s lead in low battery prices continued in 2025, with average prices in the country dropping 13% to $84/kWh. Prices in North America and Europe were 44% and 56% higher...This year, the lowest observed cell and pack prices were just $36/kWh and $50/kWh, respectively. These were for LFP batteries going into stationary storage applications.

Battery Pack Prices Fall in 2025, Despite Rising Metal Prices (source)

On December 12 Bloomberg reported:

Lithium may get much-needed demand boost from battery storage. Lithium bulls are betting on energy storage systems as the next meaningful pillar of demand for the battery metal, nudging the global market back toward balance after years of oversupply. Giant utility-scale batteries are an increasingly important consumer of lithium, with many analysts seeing demand from these storage systems rising at a faster clip than EV growth in 2026.

Note: Bold emphasis by the author.

On December 12 Fastmarkets reported:

EU’s critical minerals strategy: €3 billion boost amid industry risks...Key takeaways:

  • EU launches €3 billion plan to boost critical minerals sector and address supply chain risks.
  • Stockpiling and public-private partnerships are central to the EU’s strategy.
  • Industry experts highlight the need for investment and supply chain diversification.

On December 16 Car News China reported: "China battery company Welion achieves 824 Wh/kg energy density in lab, targets 1000 Wh/kg."

On December 17 Bloomberg reported: "Ford scraps $6.5 million EV battery contract with Korea’s LG."

On December 17 Finimize reported:

JPMorgan sees big upside for Chile’s top lithium producer...Lithium’s fortunes may be on the upswing again—at least according to JPMorgan. The bank now predicts lithium prices will bounce back 62% as large-scale energy storage projects ramp up, driven by the rapid adoption of renewables. It lifted its 2026 and 2027 price forecasts for lithium to $17,500 and $22,000 per metric ton, as battery demand grows faster than anticipated and pushes the energy storage share of total lithium demand to 42% by 2030.

On December 18 The AFR reported:

Lithium markets hit fever pitch as China revokes more permits. The euphoria pervading lithium markets has hit fever pitch this week after China revoked 27 expired permits in its major mining hub for the battery material, turbocharging a rally that has analysts scrambling to update their forecasts...While analysts said the latest cancellations of the mining permits won’t impact lithium supply, given none of them covered operating mines, the news had compounded concerns about a supply squeeze at a time when demand is booming.

On December 18 CNEVPOST reported: "CATL launches world's 1st humanoid robot-powered battery pack production line."

On December 19 Control Thermal Resources announced:

American Critical Resources and Plum Acquisition Corp. IV announce Letter of Intent for a proposed business combination...American Critical Resources was formed to advance CTR's Hell's Kitchen development at the Salton Sea in California – known as one of the world's most prolific geothermal lithium resources and considered a strategically important U.S. energy and critical minerals resource. ACR is expected to deliver approximately 50 megawatts of renewable baseload electricity and an estimated annual production of up to 100,000 metric tons of lithium carbonate over four project stages.

Note: American Critical Resources (“ACR”) is a subsidiary of Controlled Thermal Resources Holdings Inc. (“CTR”).

Lithium miner news

Albemarle (ALB)

On December 5 Seeking Alpha reported:

Albemarle upgraded to Buy at UBS as lithium seen entering new upcycle...with a $185 price target, raised from $107...UBS analyst Joshua Spector said he thinks a tighter market should lift lithium prices $2-$3/kg over the next 6-9 months, and "a market in deficit would push prices back to reinvestment levels and risk a price fly up scenario - we now think that is possible in 2027."

On December 10 Miningdotcom reported:

Albemarle wraps up validation of lithium pilot plant in Chile. Albemarle said on Wednesday it completed the validation of its direct lithium extraction (DLE) pilot plant in Chile, allowing the company to finalize its design of a future commercial facility and proceed to environmental review... Lithium recovery exceeded 94% during stable operation...Water reuse reached up to 85% during the validation phase.

Sociedad Quimica y Minera S.A. (SQM), Covalent Lithium (SQM/WES JV) - Wesfarmers [ASX:WES] (OTCPK:WFAFY)

The Mt Holland Lithium Mine and Kwinana Refinery are a 50/50 JV ("Covalent Lithium") between Wesfarmers and SQM, located in Western Australia.

On December 4 SQM announced:

Issuance and Sale of UF 10 million Local Hybrid Bond...for a total amount of UF 10,000,000 (approximately US$430 million)...The Series S Bonds (i ) mature on February 15, 2058; (ii) will accrue, on the outstanding principal expressed in UF (unidades de fomento ), a fixed annual interest rate of 4%, starting November 15, 2025.

On December 22 Kitco News reported: "Chile regulator approves Codelco-SQM lithium deal hours after announcing audit...to operate a joint lithium venture in the Atacama salt flat."

Note: Details here on the Codelco_SQM JV.

Rio Tinto [ASX:RIO] [LN:RIO] (RIO) - Acquired Arcadium Lithium in early 2025

On December 5 Rio Tinto announced:

Stronger, sharper and simpler Rio Tinto to deliver leading returns... streamlining to three world class businesses – Iron Ore, Copper and Aluminium & Lithium...Leading production growth: 7% growth expected in 20251 and 3% compound annual production growth outlook to 20302 underpinned by the delivery and ramp-up of developments across copper (Oyu Tolgoi), iron ore (Simandou) and lithium (Arcadium, Rincon).

On December 5 Rio Tinto announced:

BHP and Rio Tinto welcome first Caterpillar battery-electric haul trucks to the Pilbara...Once safely commissioned, the trials will begin to test the viability of battery-electric technology as an alternative to diesel usage in large-scale iron ore mining operations.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2025 - Plans to ramp production at their 3,000tpa Rincon Lithium Project in Argentina.
  • 2026 - Plan to expand and ramp Fenix Mine by 10,000tpa and start and ramp Sal de Vida Project to 15,000tpa LCE.
  • 2028 - Plans to start ramp of Rincon to 60,000tpa LCE and start Nemaska Lithium Project then ramp to 28,000tpa LCE.

Note: You can view a nice summary of all Rio's lithium projects and timelines here. Their end 2028 target is 225,000tpa LCE.

Ganfeng Lithium Group [SHE:002460] [HK:1772] (GNENY)

On December 17 Market Screener reported: "UBS upgrades Ganfeng Lithium Group to Buy from Neutral; Price Target is HK$63.26."

On December 22 Market Screener reported: "Lithium Royalty enters into agreement to acquire cash flowing Goulamina royalty operated by Ganfeng Lithium."

(Chengdu) Tianqi Lithium Industries Inc. [SHE:002466], Tianqi Lithium Energy Australia (TLEA) is a JV with Tianqi Lithium (51%) and IGO Limited (49%). TLEA owns the Kwinana lithium hydroxide facility in WA

On December 9 Market Screener reported: "Tianqi Lithium to issue up to 6 Billion Yuan of debt."

Chengxin Lithium Group Co Ltd [SHE:002240] - Also owns Sichuan Zhiyuan Lithium Industry Co., Ltd and is a supplier to BYD Co.

No news for the month.

Pilbara Minerals [ASX:PLS] (OTC:PILBF)

No significant news for the month apart from a Board change and a new CFO.

Mineral Resources [ASX:MIN] (MALRF)

Mineral Resources core Australian lithium assets include the Mt Marion Mine and the Wodgina Lithium Mine JV (ownership subject to final settlement in H1, 2026 - MinRes 35%, POSCO 15%, Albemarle 50%).

No significant news for the month.

Sigma Lithium Resources [TSXV:SGML] (SGMLF) (SGML)

Sigma is producing lithium spodumene at its Grota do Cirilo Mine in Brazil.

On December 1 Sigma Lithium announced:

Sigma Lithium highlights Brazil’s leadership in sustainable lithium at Cop30; Engages in high-level global dialogues on energy transition, critical minerals and ESG innovation.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2026 - Second line to be commissioned doubling production capacity to 520,000tpa spodumene once ramped. A later third stage plans to expand the mine to 125,000tpa LCE.

Liontown Limited [ASX:LTR] (OTCPK:LINRF) - formerly Liontown Resources Limited

Liontown 100% own the Kathleen Valley Lithium (spodumene) Mine in Western Australia.

On December 2 Liontown announced: "Change of company name...from Liontown Resources Limited to Liontown Limited...no change to the Company’s ASX listing code “LTR”."

On December 9 Liontown announced:

Offtake Agreement with Canmax Technologies executed...150,000 wet metric tonnes (“wmt”) per year of spodumene concentrate to be delivered over two years in 2027 and 2028. Pricing will be determined using a formula referencing spodumene concentrate indices.

On December 22 Liontown announced:

Kathleen Valley transitions to 100% underground mining with open pit operations complete...planned strategic open pit ore stockpiles enhance operational flexibility and provide feed security through to early FY27, supporting the underground transition and ramp up.

AMG Critical Materials N.V. [NA:AMG] [GR:ADG] (OTCPK:AMVMF)

On December 22 AMG announced:

AMG Lithium receives funding from German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy. AMG...is in the qualification phase before full commercial production at its lithium hydroxide refinery in Bitterfeld, Germany. After having completed ongoing customer testing of factory samples, the site will be the first industrial-scale battery-grade lithium hydroxide production facility in Europe, and by mid-2026 will operate at design capacity...AMG plans to process both technical-grade lithium salts and recycled lithium at the refinery, producing battery-grade lithium hydroxide while advancing a sustainable circular economy...this project is supported by €36 million in funding awarded to AMG Lithium by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy...In September 2024, AMG officially hosted the opening of the first module of the lithium refinery. Annual capacity of one module is 20,000 tons per year.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2025-2028 - German LiOH facility expansion plan with Modules 2-5 (100,00tpa LiOH).

Elevra Lithium [ASX:ELV] (NASDAQ:ELVR) - Formed from a merger of Sayona Mining & Piedmont Lithium

No significant news for the month.

Lithium Argentina AG [TSX:LAR](LAR) - formerly Lithium Americas Argentina

Lithium Argentina owns the Argentina assets (Cauchari Olaroz JV (44.8% share), Pastos Grandes, Sal de la Puna) from the LAC split.

No news for the month.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2025 - Stage 1 Cauchari-Olaroz lithium production expected to ramp to 30-35,000tpa in 2025. Stage 2 Cauchari-Olaroz is advancing an expansion plan considering an additional production capacity of 45,000 tpa LCE.
  • 2026-28 - Progress on the Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes lithium brine project JV (Ganfeng 67%, LAR 33%) with a Scoping Study for up to 150,000tpa LCE.

NB: Ganfeng Lithium (51%) and Lithium Argentina (49%) own the JV company Minera Exar S.A., which owns 91.5% interest and is entitled to 100% of the production from the Cauchari-Olaroz Project. The 8.5% interest is owned by Jujuy Energia y Mineria Sociedad del Estado (“JEMSE”) (a company owned by the Government of Jujuy province).

Zijin Mining Group [SHA:601899] [HKSE:2899] (ZIJMF)

Zijin Mining is a large diversified mining group with global mines focused on copper, gold, zinc/lead, silver and lithium. Zijin Mining owns 100% of the 3Q Lithium Mine in Argentina, with Stage 1 production started producing 2,903t LCE in 2023 and planning to ramp to 25,000tpa LCE. Zijin has other lithium assets and large production growth plans.

On December 12 Asian Metal reported:

Zijin Mining commissions first hard-rock lithium polymetallic project...at the Xiangyuan lithium polymetallic deposit in Daoxian, Hunan...with production capacity of 30,000 tonnes per year battery-grade lithium carbonate.

Eramet [FR:ERA] (OTCPK:ERMAY) (OTCPK:ERMAF)

Eramet owns the Centenario-Ratones Lithium Mine in Argentina.

On December 4 Eramet announced:

Eramet: taking decisive actions to address continued challenging market conditions and unlock value...Centenario lithium ramp-up on track, targeting production rate at c.90% of design capacity by mid-2026; volume impact to contribute on top of the potential EBITDA uplift presented above.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2025 - Ramp up of lithium production in Argentina. Target to reach production of 10-13kt LCE in 2025.

POSCO [KRX:005490] (PKX)

POSCO owns the Sal de Oro Project in the Hombre Muerto salar, Argentina. Some details here. POSCO also has a JV with Pilbara Minerals on a 43ktpa lithium hydroxide facility in South Korea which commenced operations in 2024.

On December 8 Lithium South announced:

Signing of Acceptance Letter for Share Purchase Agreement with POSCO Argentina S.A.U...Terms of the sale are a cash price of US$65 million before taxes, payment of the Hydra X and XI claims and other closing costs.

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2025 - Target to commence production at Hombre Muerto and ramp to 25ktpa LiOH, then later an additional 25ktpa lithium carbonate.

Core Lithium Ltd. [ASX:CXO] [GR:7CX] (CXOXF) (CORX)

Core 100% owns the Finniss Lithium Project (Grants Resource) in Northern Territory Australia. The Project is currently not in production due to low prices.

On December 23 Core Lithium announced: "Sale of non-core uranium assets for $5M."

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2025 - Any resumption of mining operations at the Grants open pit at Finniss where mining has been suspended since Jan. 2024.

Argosy Minerals [ASX:AGY][GR:AM1] (ARYMF)

Argosy Minerals 77.5% owns (option to increase to 90%) the Rincon Lithium Mine in Argentina. Their 2,000tpa lithium facility is currently suspended.

No significant news for the month.

Upcoming catalysts:

  • 2025+ - Work on the next stage (total 12,000tpa) project at Rincon Lithium Project. Any re-opening of the suspended 2,000tpa operations at Rincon Lithium Mine.

Other Chinese lithium producers (several are not listed)

  • Zhicun Lithium Group Ltd (private)
  • Sichuan Yahua Industrial Group Co Ltd [SHE:002497]
  • Jianxiawo (owned by CATL)
  • Jiangxi Nanshi Lithium Battery New Material Co., Ltd (owned by Nanshi Group and Yichun Mining)
  • Jinzhou Yongshan Lithium [SHA:603399]
  • Sinomine Resource Group [SHE:002738]
  • Canmax [SHE:300390]
  • Huayou Cobalt [SHA:603799]

Lithium miner ETFs

  • Sprott Lithium Miners ETF (LITP) - A pure play lithium ETF
  • Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT)
  • ProShares S&P Global Core Battery Metals ETF (ION)
  • The Amplify Lithium & Battery Technology ETF (BATT)

Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT) 5 year price chart (source)

Trend Investing lithium demand v supply model forecasts

Trend Investing forecasts lithium demand to increase 10.4x this decade.

Note: A Nov. 2020 UBS forecast is for "lithium demand to lift 11-fold from ~400kt in 2021 through to 2030."

Conclusion

China lithium carbonate spot prices and spodumene spot prices were significantly higher the past month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Shipments of #BESS cells are surging in 2025, set to exceed 550 GWh this year, a 76% y-o-y increase.
  • U.S. Export-Import Bank plans to spend $100B for critical minerals, energy dominance...Nuclear energy will be a focus.
  • Tianqi Lithium expects global lithium demand to reach 2 million tonnes of LCE by 2026, essentially achieving a supply-demand balance.
  • Battery pack prices fall in 2025 down 8% to $108/kWh, despite rising metal prices. The lowest observed cell and pack prices were just $36/kWh and $50/kWh used for LFP batteries going into stationary storage applications.
  • Lithium bulls are betting on energy storage systems as the next meaningful pillar of demand for the battery metal, nudging the global market back toward balance after years of oversupply.
  • EU launches €3 billion plan to boost critical minerals sector. Stockpiling and public-private partnerships are central to the EU’s strategy.
  • Ford scraps $6.5 million EV battery contract with Korea’s LG.
  • JP Morgan predicts lithium prices will bounce back 62% as large-scale energy storage projects ramp up. It lifted its 2026 and 2027 price forecasts for lithium to $17,500/t and $22,000/t.
  • Lithium markets hit fever pitch as China revokes more permits.
  • American Critical Resources (aka CTR) and Plum Acquisition Corp. IV announce Letter of Intent for a proposed business combination.
  • UBS sees a lithium market in deficit is possible in 2027.
  • Albemarle upgraded to Buy at UBS as lithium seen entering new upcycle, assigns a $185 price target. Wraps up validation of DLE pilot plant in Chile.
  • SQM sells US$430M of 35 year local hybrid bonds at a fixed 4% rate. Chile regulator approves Codelco-SQM lithium deal.
  • Rio's strategy - 3% compound annual production growth outlook to 20302 underpinned by the delivery and ramp-up of developments across copper (Oyu Tolgoi), iron ore (Simandou) and lithium (Arcadium, Rincon).
  • Tianqi Lithium to issue up to 6 Billion Yuan of debt.
  • Liontown Resources Limited changes name to Liontown Limited. Signs 150ktpa offtake agreement with Canmax Technologies for 2027 & 2028.
  • AMG Lithium receives funding from German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Energy.
  • Zijin Mining commissions first hard-rock lithium polymetallic project in China, 30,000tpa LCE capacity.
  • Eramet's Centenario lithium ramp-up on track, targeting production rate at c.90% of design capacity by mid-2026.
  • POSCO signs acceptance letter to purchase Lithium South's HMN Project for US$65M.

As usual all comments are welcome.

data.title: Economic Growth In The U.S.: Doing Well
data.description:
The U.S. economy experienced an annualized 4.3 percent rate of growth in the third quarter of 2025.

This high of a rate of growth was not expected and represented the highest growth rate of the U.S. economy in two years. Note that this rate is a quarterly rate, annualized, to get a yearly rate.

The year-over-year rate of growth of real GDP came in at 2.3 percent.

This rate of growth fits right in with how the U.S. economy has been doing over the past 15 years or so.

Take a look at the long-term picture of U.S. economic growth.

Also, when one calculates the compound rate of economic growth from the summer of 2009 through the third quarter of 2025, one comes up with an economic growth figure of 2.4 percent.

In the summer of 2009, the Federal Reserve System, under the leadership of Chairman Ben Bernanke, established a "new" approach to monetary policy, a policy based upon the "quantitative" change in the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve.

This approach to monetary policy basically established a "supply side" growth pattern for the U.S. economy, and this supply-side growth model has dominated the Fed's results ever since. And the compound for the past 15 years includes the wide swings in the economy that are associated with the spread of the Covid-19 pandemic and the short economic recession that took place in 2020.

I have written many posts supporting this conclusion over the past year or so.

The result...the 2.4 percent compound rate of growth of real GDP going back to 2009.

The conclusion one can draw from this result is that the U.S. economy is continuing its "strong" performance generated by the steady monetary policy of the Federal Reserve System.

Note that the third-quarter data may still face substantial revisions because they have been assembled during a time when the U.S. statistical system was not open.

What about inflation?

Inflation, as measured by the PCE Index...the Personal Consumption Expenditure Index...rose at an annual rate of 2.9 percent in the third quarter of 2025. This is up from 2.6 percent in the second quarter...and is substantially higher than the 2.0 annual rate that the Federal Reserve has been shooting for.

It seems to be apparent that the U.S. economy is producing a much stronger performance than a lot of people expected.

As far as economic growth is concerned, economic growth certainly seems to be doing much better than the Trump administration thought it would be doing at this stage.

The question then has to be asked...do you think that there is any chance that the Trump administration will "back off" a little bit from the "aggressive" plan for economic stimulus it has promoted since the day it was elected?

My bet is that the Trump administration will not "back off" from pushing the aggressive stimulus program it has talked about...which includes getting a new, more aggressive Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, a Chairman that will be a lot more likely to reduce the Federal Reserve policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate...by two or more percentage points.

Trump wants to push for faster economic growth.

His view...the way to get faster economic growth is to lower the Fed's policy rate of interest.

This effort will upset the "Bernanke" program, a program that has seemingly worked very well over the past fifteen years or so.

The program will spur on greater inflation.

And the program will disrupt the "supply side" approach to economic growth and will result in a more volatile economy.

This is my expectation.

We'll see.

One other point.

As my readers know, I much prefer to write about the year-over-year rate of growth of the real economy and not the "annualized" rate of growth of the real economy.

The quarterly numbers in the third quarter of 2025 show how the two approaches can give off substantially different pictures of how the economy is doing.

Which number represents the third quarter of 2025 the best?

The U.S. economy grew by an annualized 4.3 percent.

Or,

The U.S. economy grew by a year-over-year rate of growth of 2.3 percent.

The one number is almost twice the amount of the other number.

Which number gives the most appropriate picture of the existing situation?

I go with the year-over-year number in any case. To me, a year-over-year number gives me the most consistent picture of what the real situation is.

To me, the annualized number can be dominated by multiplying the actual "quarterly" number by 4 and providing a result that is a greater relative number than what is really achieved.

Saying that the U.S. economy grew by 4.3 percent in the third quarter of 2025 really makes it look like the economy is growing very rapidly.

Saying the U.S. economy grew by 2.3 percent in the third quarter of 2025 gives us a more realistic picture of what is going on in the economy and does not make it look like the economy is "taking off."

data.title: Allspring Utility And Telecommunications Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Utility & Telecommunications Fund increased 6.40% on a total return basis in the third quarter versus an increase of 7.57% for the S&P 500 Utilities Index benchmark.
  • Utilities holdings outperformed the benchmark.
  • Collectively, the fund's allocation to telecommunications and other sectors underperformed the benchmark.

Market review

Utilities' total return slightly lagged that of the S&P 500 Index during the quarter, and the sector remains neck and neck with the index year to date. Investors generally continued to favor riskier stocks, and the broader market made all-time highs despite meaningful geopolitical and economic concerns. As a result, several defensive sectors have lagged this year. However, utilities have performed well, driven by consistent and improving fundamentals, attractive valuations, and increased interest in companies that can produce power to supply data center expansion.

We believe the typically defensive utilities sector remains well positioned for whatever macroeconomic scenarios play out for the rest of the year. If the U.S. experiences an economic slowdown, we think regulated utilities' low volatility and steady, predictable earnings and dividend growth would be highly attractive to investors. Valuation looks attractive as well. The sector recently traded at approximately a 20% discount versus the broader market. We also continue to believe utilities are better positioned for growth than in recent memory. The U.S. looks like it has entered an era of rising electricity demand unseen in decades. Signs of demand growth emerged in 2023 and have since strengthened. Utilities across the country have raised growth forecasts. Beyond the much-discussed proliferation of data centers, utilities have also noted growing electric vehicle penetration and the reshoring of manufacturing to the U.S. as factors driving higher electricity usage. We believe more utilities could raise earnings guidance if demand growth further materializes.

Figures quoted represent past performance, which is no guarantee of future results, and do not reflect taxes a shareholder may pay on an investment in a fund. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund's website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund's gross expense ratio is 0.84%. The fund's net expense ratio is 0.72%.

The manager has contractually committed, through July 31, 2026, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund's total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.72% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund's returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio positioning Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

  • The fund's utility holdings outperformed the benchmark for the quarter, benefiting from not owning Consolidated Edison, Inc. (ED), and NRG Energy Inc. (NRG). ED operates in New York, which the managers view as a below-average regulatory environment for utilities, and we believe the company has below-average growth prospects. NRG shares, meanwhile, took a breather in the quarter, underperforming the index after significant outperformance in the first half. The other independent power producers (IPPs), a couple of which the fund does own, followed this pattern. The IPPs have all benefited from the theme of increasing demand for electricity. The largest overall contributor to relative performance was the position in Alphabet Inc. Class A (GOOGL), which rallied in our view due to a milder-than-expected antitrust ruling that avoided harsh remedies coupled with accelerating momentum in its artificial intelligence (AI) offerings, particularly in advertising and cloud services. Other telecommunications-related holdings detracted from performance in the quarter.
  • The fund invests a portion of the portfolio outside of the utilities and telecommunications sectors. These holdings overall underperformed the benchmark in the quarter. Financials holdings Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Inc. (MA) performed poorly on concerns around consumer spending trends and rising operating expenses. Health care holdings UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) and Amgen Inc. (AMGN) also underperformed the benchmark—in part, we think, due to the market's continued preference for riskier stocks. The managers sold the position in UNH after the company announced a CEO change, suspended financial guidance, and reportedly became the subject of a Department of Justice investigation into Medicare billing practices. We continue to believe that maintaining an allocation to non-utility and telecom stocks that offer what we expect to be solid, above-average dividend growth can benefit both overall portfolio dividend growth and total return over time.

Portfolio positioning - Outlook

  • The Inflation Reduction Act has created uncertainty for clean energy spending by rolling back certain tax incentives. While some projects may be delayed or canceled, we believe the ongoing transition to cleaner generation facilities is likely to continue. We think the demand growth many utilities are now forecasting may require an “all of the above” need for new generation capacity: gas, renewables, nuclear, and the delay of coal plant retirements. This should require robust spending on both new generation and expansion of the transmission and distribution grid. Even with a phasing out of tax credits, renewable generation seems well positioned given cost and speed-to-market advantages relative to natural gas and potential new nuclear facilities.
  • Increasing capital spending is an important challenge for the sector. While capital investments drive earnings and dividend growth for utilities, they also put pressure on customer bills. As a result, regulators could choose to limit incremental capital spending to mitigate bill increases. We do not think regulators will meaningfully constrain investment, however, as the majority target the safety and reliability of systems and accommodate the addition of new customers. Furthermore, we think most utilities have done an admirable job limiting expense growth to offset the effect of additional capital spending on customer bills.
  • Much of the telecommunications sector operates in mature, slow-growth markets, including television broadcasting and entertainment, internet access, wireless communications and infrastructure, and data networking. The managers do not expect the environment to change meaningfully in the near term, although longer term, emerging use cases in areas like 5G wireless technology, cloud computing, and AI could improve growth prospects. The managers have reduced exposure to these slower-growing markets. By adding positions in META and GOOGL shares, the fund has begun to tilt toward faster-growing communications applications.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The S&P 500 Utilities Index is a market-value-weighted index that measures the performance of all stocks within the utilities sector of the S&P 500 Index. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks:

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Funds that concentrate their investments in limited sectors, such as utilities and telecommunication services, are more vulnerable to adverse market, economic, regulatory, political, or other developments affecting those sectors. Non-diversified funds are more vulnerable to market or economic events impacting issuers of individual portfolio securities than a diversified fund. The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

data.title: NioCorp Developments: Pre-Production, But Worth A Place In A Long-Term Portfolio
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I remember when rare earth minerals and mining companies were all the rage in the market news a few months ago as the US government began to invest in a few of them to lock up and secure supply lines. Well, now a lot of them have come down in price, and quite a lot too. NioCorp Developments (NB), for instance, peaked at just under $12 per share in October, now trading below $6 per share. Has anything fundamental changed between then and now for the company? The short answer is no. It's still pre-revenue and in the development stage for its Nebraska-based mining project focusing on Niobium, Scandium, and Titanium.

The company has all the qualities to be a long-term solid compounder. It's an investment in future demand for rare minerals and US dependency on other countries. For me, this is a solid Strong Buy right now in the market, especially after the pullback from the October highs.

Pre-Revenue - All Going to Plan So Far

As I mentioned in the first segment, NB is still pre-revenue but is progressing quite well. The company has managed to secure a lot of funding and contracts for the minerals being produced there, all before production has even started. The financing is a mix between equity and debt. Equity financing has contributed $380 million so far this year. In terms of share dilution, that comes out to a 65.7% increase, from 45.9 million to 76.1 million. Given that the stock price is also up over 300% this year, I think early investors might not care that much, though, to be frank. Investors who bought at the top in October might view it differently. But then again, the numbers were quite public at that point as to what the mine was going to produce over its 38-year expected lifetime.

Minerals - End Use and More Crucial Info

Processed Into

End Use

Market Growth

Supply Line

Niobium

Ferroniobium

Improve steel quality, pipelines, and solid-state lithium-ion batteries

Lithium-ion batteries - 23.22% CAGR until 2032.

Brazil controls 97% of the world's exploitable reserves.

Scandium

Scandium Oxide

Create ultra-strong aluminum alloys, aircraft frames, defense systems, and solid oxide fuel cells.

Aircraft frames - 5.8% CAGR until 2032. Oxide batteries - 31.2% CAGR until 2030.

China mostly, historically also Ukraine and Russia as a byproduct from uranium mining.

Titanium

Titanium Tetrachloride

White pigment, aerospace-grade alloys, medical implants.

Medical implants - 6.64% until 2030.

China and Australia hold over 70% of global reserves.

I've compiled above a table to show the main three minerals that the mine NB is working will be producing at first. There are a few others, but these are the first and most important ones. As the table shows, they are used in a lot of high-growth end markets. As with most of these mineral markets these days, the most crucial ones are end markets like batteries, EV usage, and semiconductors.

Worth knowing is that the US does import most of these minerals, if not all, because production is so small. Titanium, for instance, had only about 200 tonnes produced in the US, with reserves in the range of 2,000 tonnes. So not a large market. Scandium holds more importance as it's led to $10 million in funding from the Pentagon, as well as a partnership with Lockheed Martin (LMT). The US has for a long time been reliant on foreign producers of this, mostly China, Russia, and Ukraine, where it's a byproduct in other mining operations.

When NioCorp comes online to produce approximately 100 tonnes per year of scandium oxide – after being 100% dependent upon foreign producers

One site puts the price per kg of scandium at $3,197, meaning the expected production from Elk Creek would generate $31.97 million per year, based on that figure in the quote.

The other mineral would be Niobium, which puts NB in the unique position to be one of the larger domestic producers of this. But it's still not the largest. Iron Hill is shown to be the largest in the US, but there isn't an active project here, it seems. It's owned by Teck Resources (TECK), and there is a push by Canamera Energy Metals (EMETF) to start production here, but the mining claims are still unpatented and pending BLM registration.

Round Top, however, is being developed by USA Rare Earth (USAR) and TMRC in a joint partnership. It's still in pre-production, though. In Cave Peak there is mostly exploration, but no real push, it seems, to develop it at a faster pace and get it operational.

Processed Into

End Use

Market Growth

Supply Line

Neodymium-praseodymium

NdPr Oxide - NdFeB permanent magnets

EV motors, wind turbines, and smartphones

Wind turbines - 10.63% until 2030. EVs - 16.12% until 2032

China holds the largest reserves and largest refining capabilities.

Dysprosium

Dysprosium Oxide - added to NdFeB magnets

High-temperature magnets for EV motors, wind turbines, defense systems, and hard drives.

Defense systems - 5.12% until 2030.

Hard drives - 6.48% until 2030.

China holds the largest amount of refining and processing capabilities

Terbium

Terbium Oxide - Green phosphors

LED displays, lightning, lasers, fuel cells

LED Displays - 5.72% until 2030. Lasers - 7.06% until 2030.

Zero US production so far.

Above are the minerals that the company plans on expanding into as the mine progresses. For those that follow the mining industry, you might be familiar with a few of these commodities. For those who have read about the company MP Materials (MP) should definitely be aware of Neodymium. It's perhaps the biggest material being produced in their mine, Mountain Pass in California. What differentiates this company from NB is that it is in full production and is also now utilizing its own refinement plant. Historically, China has been the place where you ship your minerals to get processed and refined and then shipped back again. This is changing now, and companies like MP are trying to set up fully domestic supply chains instead. NB will still have to rely on China for refinement purposes, which means that the relationship between the US and China could impact its ability to handle the products. This is a serious risk consideration.

Elk Creek Long-Term Outlook

In a presentation from 2022, the company shows the current expected life span of the mine, the products mined there, and the reserves as well. In short, it has an expected life span of 38 years, mining 946,000 tonnes per year.

NB included an operating cost structure, which puts the total annual Opex at around $196 million per year, based on the 946,000 tonnes mined per year/throughput. In the company's earlier presentation, it did not include the REE in the revenues, as there was no feasibility study for this. Since the report was in 2022, and the prices have changed quite a bit, the old model suggests far higher revenues today than anticipated back then. For instance, the price of Ferroniobium was between $50 and $60 per kg in 2024, compared to the model in the presentation using $39.6.

In the company's more recent presentations, however, it does include a figure of $21.9 billion in gross revenues over the entire mine life, with a 68% average annual EBITDA margin as well ($14.892 billion EBITDA). All the permits are approved for the project, but the actual construction is yet to start. A conservative estimate would be about 3-4 years until the actual production could start, with perhaps 6 months for the ramp-up to finish. So we're looking at $403 million in EBITDA by 2032 at least. With an EV of $521 million right now, this puts the company at an FWD EV/EBITDA of 1.29. Considering the sector median is 9.17 on an FWD basis, this shows a serious upside potential in the medium term. At the 9x mark, it implies an EV of $3.6 billion, a 691% increase from today.

The issue that some might see here is that it will essentially increase to this figure and then stay flat. Meaning, are there any other expansions the company could make at the current location to increase the mine life or create new revenue streams? Being dependent on one location is a risk, of course, and there will be considerable volatility because revenues are also very tied to market prices.

Back in September of this year, the company performed several drilling tests at the site, which potentially could drastically increase the size of the reserves. Because these aren't yet confirmed and seem to be more about speculation, I'll focus on confirmed figures. There is still considerable value based on the confirmed figures from the company, so sticking with that seems better for the sake of this article.

The table above is trying to figure out the additional revenues that the rare earth minerals would add for the company.

(does not include rare earth production, metallurgical advancements, and mine plan improvements)

As the quote says from the last presentation, these REEs aren't accounted for in the feasibility study that NB has made. But it's clear that there would be considerable benefits from accounting for it. The revenue figures are again from the 2022 report. At the same EBITDA margin, the annual addition would be roughly $265.5 million per year, leading to nearly $700 million in annual EBITDA if the figures and estimates that NB has made are true. This increases the upside potential even further, but for me, crucially, also reduces the risk profile here by a wide margin. Even when not accounting for rare earth minerals, the project looks very attractive over the long term. It's, of course, a more risk-adjusted bet since there is no production yet. I'd assume that any halt or setback in getting production up will severely hit the share price. But because of the long-term positive revenue and profitability image here, I think the risk is worth it.

I've talked about the valuation a little already; even if it gets pushed out 1 or 2 years, the upside once operations are normalized and NB supposedly is generating $403 million, potentially $668 million in annual EBITDA, having an EV of just $521 million for me implies a serious undervaluation compared to the long-term value of the Elk Creek.

Risks

The main risk that revolves around the company right now is the project and construction being delayed further. Since no revenues have been generated yet and it relies on a mix of debt and equity financing, it would dilute, and frankly most likely will, shareholders a lot more before the first revenues are recorded. Because of the backing from the Pentagon, the Exim Bank as well, which is processing the $780 million debt financing application.

Because NB has already secured customer lines for scandium, for example, with LMT, that partially offsets some of the risks of having to sell on the spot market. Dilution and increased leverage are the main risks right now. With $162 million in cash, it can sustain at least 13 more quarters of operating expenses, based on the $12 million total reported for the last period.

The Full Picture

NB is a different play in the minerals industry, as it is a pure US-based company with serious growth prospects at its Elk Creek location. It has a large reserve of crucial materials that are used in a number of different end products, most of which are projected to show steady and consistent growth over the next few years.

If the company can manage to realize the figures from the mine feasibility study, showing $403 million in annual EBITDA and $265 million more if the rare earth minerals are accounted for, well then the company is trading for pennies on the dollar, so to speak. I think that's a fair statement considering the EV is below $550 million and we could be 4-5 years away from the mine being in full production. The company has a solid cash balance as well and support from the government too. It checks all the boxes for me as a speculative growth play. Rating NB shares a Strong Buy.

data.title: John Hancock 2010 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Positive investment conditions led to broad-based gains for the financial markets in the third quarter.
  • The fund, while positive in absolute terms, didn't keep pace with its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation was a modest detractor.

Market review and outlook

Financial assets delivered strong returns in the quarter, with both stocks and bonds registering gains. A favorable backdrop of positive global growth, falling interest rates across the developed markets, and a lack of pronounced negative news fueled a surge in investors' appetite for risk. Most notably, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by a quarter point in September, and the markets anticipated that additional easing could be in store over the months ahead. Investors were further cheered by positive corporate earnings reports and the relatively limited initial economic impact from tariffs.

These developments helped propel major equity indexes to a series of all-time highs. The United States led the way higher behind renewed enthusiasm about the artificial intelligence theme and the technology sector more broadly. Small-cap stocks, which benefited from the improving interest rate outlook and investors' preference for risk, performed particularly well. The emerging markets were another top performer, with China registering an impressive gain amid revived optimism about the country's economic outlook.

The global fixed-income markets also produced positive returns, reflecting the increasingly favorable shift in Fed policy. Longer-term debt and credit-oriented categories generally provided the best results.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation was a slight detractor from the fund's relative performance in the quarter. An underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks and corresponding overweights in defensive equities, mid caps, and the international markets were the primary drivers of the modest shortfall. An overweight in bonds, while consistent with the fund's mandate, also hurt results. On the other hand, diversification into a real assets portfolio—which was helped by its position in metals and mining stocks—contributed to performance. An overweight in emerging-market bonds in the fixed-income portfolio was a further positive.

Portfolio changes

Investment conditions remained broadly positive at the close of the period, highlighted by ongoing optimism related to economic conditions and global central bank policy. However, valuations have become fairly full across many areas of the equity and fixed-income markets. With this in mind, we maintain a diversified portfolio designed to hold up through a wide range of possible outcomes. We think it's better to keep a long-term view rather than trying to chase returns in specific categories. We believe this approach should hold the fund in good stead over time, and we think its merits may become increasingly apparent if the market backdrop becomes less favorable than investors are anticipating.

data.title: Invesco Limited Term California Municipal Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Fund performance

Invesco Limited Term California Municipal Fund Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) underperformed its style-specific index, the S&P Municipal California Investment Grade 4-7 Years Bond Index.

2 Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

3 Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In the third quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 2.87%, 1.58% and 2.66%, respectively. 1 The municipal market saw mixed performance: July and August continued the previous quarter's underperformance, but September brought strong performance in long duration municipals as US Treasuries rallied and municipal bond fund flows increased.
  • New municipal bond issuance reached $153 billion in the third quarter, bringing the year-to-date total to $437 billion. This is 13% higher than the same period in 2024 and significantly above previous years. 1
  • Municipal fund net flows were a highlight, totaling about $16.2 billion for the quarter and $31.8 billion year to date. 2
  • In September, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) made its first rate cut of the year, lowering the federal funds rate by 0.25%. The Fed cited increased downside risks to employment and reiterated its commitment to balancing its dual mandate of maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. Future policy decisions will depend on incoming data, the evolving outlook and the balance of risks. 3
  • We believe state and municipal budgets remain healthy. Although the pace of credit rating upgrades has slowed, credit fundamentals still appear strong and upgrades have outpaced downgrades so far this year. 4
  • The quarter ended with the threat of a government shutdown as Congress struggled to pass funding bills needed to keep federal agencies operating in the fiscal year beginning October 1st. 1
  • During periods of policy uncertainty, we rely on our experienced credit research team to identify and capitalize on marketplace dislocations in order to deliver value to shareholders.
  • Looking ahead, we see opportunities in the municipal bond market. With potential for further Fed rate cuts and steady new municipal issuance, we believe high absolute yields and strong fundamentals make municipals an appealing option.

Portfolio positioning

Yield Curve and Duration Positioning: Overall, we maintain a constructive outlook for duration and are looking to selectively add longer duration securities to the fund when appropriate and according to prospectus guidelines. We maintain an overweight in the shorter maturity segment of the yield curve given the fund's short duration mandate.

Sector Allocations: We maintain an overweight position in revenue bonds relative to state and local general obligation bonds. Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the airport, public power and higher education sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market as upgrades of credit ratings have continued to outpace downgrades. Various sectors have been under pressure due to uncertainty surrounding federal funding and tariff policy, as well as higher labor costs. We are monitoring these sectors closely and continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to find pockets of opportunity.

Performance highlights

The third quarter had a volatile start as record levels of new issuance and uncertainty related to fiscal and monetary policies weighed on municipal valuations. Momentum shifted in late August as labor market weakness fueled expectations that the Fed would resume monetary easing. Municipals rallied strongly through September and ended the quarter with positive returns. As expected, the Fed cut the federal funds rates by 0.25% in September and in its comments on future actions indicated a stance favoring easier monetary policy. Yields fell across the municipal yield curve, with yields on two- and five-year bonds down 0.35% and 0.41%, respectively. 5 Higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed for the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

The fund's exposure to bonds with effective durations between nine and 15 years added to relative return during the quarter. An underweight allocation to state general obligation bonds also contributed to relative performance.

Detractors from performance

Detractors from relative return this quarter included the following:

The fund's overweight in bonds with durations shorter than one year detracted from relative performance, as did security selection among bonds with durations between four and five years. Security selection in industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR) bonds and toll roads also detracted from relative performance.

data.title: Harbor Convertible Securities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The backdrop of continuing U.S. labor market weakness has been increasingly reflected in consumer sentiment surveys and upward trends in credit delinquencies. - BlueCove Limited

Market in Review

The risk rally continued during the third quarter, with U.S. equities gaining 8% and U.S. convertibles up nearly 9%. Though overall market volatility was muted, ongoing headlines in the Technology sector, which accounts for about 30% of the U.S. convertible bond market, provided a compelling opportunity set for the Harbor Convertible Securities Fund's ("Fund") security-selection emphasis.

Portfolio Performance

During the third quarter of 2025, the Harbor Convertible Securities Fund (Institutional Class) returned 9.07%, outperforming its benchmark, the ICE BofA US Convertible Index, which returned 8.58%.

Returns from security selection were positive, driven by names in the Technology sector. The performance of BlueCove's proprietary research insights were positive, led by sentiment signals. The strongest returns came in the medium (at-the-money) delta convertibles, but performance was also positive for low and high delta convertibles.

Retirement Class shares commenced operations on March 1, 2016. The performance attributed to the Retirement Class shares prior to that date is that of the Institutional Class shares. Performance prior to March 1, 2016 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of Retirement Class shares. During this period, Retirement Class shares would have had returns similar to, but somewhat higher than, Institutional Class shares due to the fact that Retirement Class shares represent interests in the same portfolio as Institutional Class shares but are subject to lower expenses.

Underweights and Overweights

The Fund seeks to be broadly neutral in duration, yield curve, and equity delta exposure versus the index. During the quarter, the largest sector positions were an overweight to Capital Goods and an underweight to Electrics and Consumer Cyclicals.

Since a large portion of the convertible bond universe is not rated, the Fund does not have a specific credit-quality bias. Risk continues to be focused on security selection in convertible bonds, with the Fund well-matched on delta exposures versus the benchmark. We seek to invest only in index-eligible securities and U.S. dollar securities, and we do not emphasize country selection versus the benchmark.

Outlook

The backdrop of continuing U.S. labor market weakness has been increasingly reflected in consumer sentiment surveys and upward trends in credit delinquencies. We believe these factors underpin concerns that expensive risky asset valuations belie macroeconomic fundamentals. BlueCove's U.S. Economic Regime Indicator shifted from "Contraction" to "Slowdown" in August, due to diminished risk of a trade war tipping the economy into recession. However, considering the labor market, our indicator remains close to the threshold that flags elevated recessionary risk. Were it to summon an increase in market volatility, we would expect this to be a positive backdrop for a scientific approach to security selection in the Fund, which seeks to capture this volatility through company-specific insights.

Risks

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Core Plus Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The third quarter of 2025 was marked by shifts in macroeconomic, geopolitical, fundamental, and supply and demand dynamics. Markets rebounded from the lows following the “Liberation Day” announcements as immediate economic impacts remained subdued. The final outcomes for tariffs and other policies remain unknown, as do their longer-term economic impacts. However, financial markets gained confidence over the quarter that the economy remained solid, inflation remained rangebound, and geopolitical risks showed early signs of easing.

Economic data in the U.S. continued to point to a resilient economy. Growth improved after a weak first half, with inflation staying contained and unemployment holding near historically low levels despite some downside surprises. This allowed the Federal Reserve (Fed) to resume easing policy with a 25-basis-point (BPS) reduction at the September Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting. The Fed has acknowledged that while elements of its mandate are in conflict, it has chosen to focus on protecting the labor market in the near term. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan. Geopolitical developments were mixed during the third quarter, with volatility re-emerging in the Russia/Ukraine conflict following the Alaska summit in August, while the Middle East saw some signs of progress.

The continuing market rebound resulted in generally positive financial market performance in the quarter. Fixed income sector performance was also positive, with interest rates lower during the period. Most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries, and the U.S. Treasury curve steepened slightly. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased by 11 bps, the 5-year Treasury yield decreased by 6 bps, the 10-year Treasury yield decreased by 8 bps, and the 30-year Treasury yield moved 4 bps lower.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund (Class I) returned 2.16% in the third quarter versus the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index return of 2.03%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund's underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact, as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and had positive excess returns.
  • Emerging Markets (EM) Sovereign: The Fund's overweight and selection in the EM sovereign sector was positive.
  • Investment Grade (IG) Corporates Selection: Issue selection versus the benchmark was positive for the period.

Performance Detractors

  • Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS): Allocation to RMBS over agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and selection within RMBS had a negative impact on performance.
  • Bank Loans Selection: Issue selection was negative.

Current Fund Strategy and Positioning

  • Increased exposure to MBS, EM corporate bonds, and EM local currency bonds.
  • Reduced RMBS, non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities, and cash.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund's sector allocation during the quarter, we continue to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

EM Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index returned 4.75% in 3Q, with EM high yield (HY) outperforming Em Ig. EM corporate debt returned 3.18%. In 3Q, the U.S. imposed a 50% tariff on Brazilian exports, straining ties and pushing Brazil toward China. Former president Jair Bolsonaro was convicted for attempting a coup after his 2022 loss. In Argentina, President Javier Milei's party suffered a major defeat to Peronists in Buenos Aires, sparking market volatility. Later, the U.S. pledged $20 billion in aid to support Milei's reforms. In Ecuador, President Noboa ended diesel subsidies, triggering widespread protests as fuel prices surged. The Fund's overall EM exposure was higher throughout the quarter. The Fund maintained a preference for U.S. dollar assets and Em Hy over Em Ig.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads ended 3Q in the low 70s—the lowest levels since 1998—while yields moved lower by 18 bps to 4.81%. Despite lower spreads and yields, IG saw positive fund flows in every week of 3Q. Institutional buyers remain active as well. Though supply for the quarter was heavier than the five-year average, it was easily absorbed. Upgrades outpaced downgrades year-to-date, though at a slower pace than in recent years. BBBs are at their lowest percentage of the overall market in a decade. We are focused on defensive industries given tight valuations, with a preference for utilities, pipelines, and capital goods.

HY Corporates: The HY market, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index, gained 2.54% in 3Q. Credit spreads tightened by 23 bps to +267 bps. Stronger-than-expected economic growth, a dovish Fed pivot that produced a 25-bps rate cut in September, resilient corporate earnings, and significant inflows contributed to performance. Media outperformed due to anticipated deregulation and positive merger and acquisition developments. Chemicals and packaging underperformed due to weak global demand.

Securitized: The securitized markets had positive total and excess returns for 3Q. Spreads tightened despite elevated issuance across all securitized sub-sectors as investors sought out additional income and spread within securitized sectors. We added agency MBS during the quarter as we felt the sector had lagged alternatives such as the IG corporate sector. While our strategy continues to focus on up-in-quality given signs of stress on the margin for certain borrowers, we're also selectively exploring individual transactions that present promising yield opportunities lower in the capital stack.

data.title: Gabelli SRI Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Social Responsible Investing

Socially responsible investing (SRI) refers to the proactive screening of companies that meet predetermined social guidelines. Specifically, the Fund excludes companies that derive 10% or more of their revenues from tobacco, cannabis, alcohol, gambling, or weapons production. This screen is relatively straightforward and transparent, incorporating a broad range of social criteria that have been utilized by asset owners for decades. The Fund otherwise relies on the Advisors' Private Market Value with a Catalyst™ approach to selecting underpriced securities. By incorporating values-based screens, the Fund may be able to minimize the risks associated with the identified industries, improving the overall return of the investment portfolio.

Investment Scorecard

The top contributor to the Fund in the third quarter was Resideo (REZI) (1.2% of net assets as of September 30, 2025; +96%), whose shares soared after the company reported better than expected second quarter results and announced it would pay $1.59 billion to permanently resolve the legacy environmental liabilities it assumed in conjunction with its spin-off from Honeywell (HON). With these liabilities resolved, the company was able to announce the planned separation of its ADI Global Distribution business and its Products and Solutions business into two separate public companies. Shares of global water solutions leader Xylem (XYL) (3.9%; +14%) rose, as the company reported solid Q2 results with revenue up 6% and earnings up 16%, driven by strength in its Measurement and Control Solutions and Water Solutions and Services businesses. Cavco (CVCO) (1.5%; +34%) shares climbed as it reported a 56% increase in fiscal first quarter EPS as demand for affordable manufactured homes remained healthy, and the company's insurance operations benefited from lower weather-related claims. The company also completed the acquisition of American Homestar Corp., which adds two manufacturing facilities and nineteen retail stores, and is expected to be accretive to earnings and cash flow. Finally, shares of Sony Group (SONY) (3.5%; +13%) gained on strong results in gaming and music; the company completed the spin-off of its Financial Services business just after quarter end.

Detractors included BellRing Brands (BRBR) (1.7%; -37%), whose shares again fell on investor concerns about increasing competition and slowing growth in the protein shake category; we continue to believe BellRing is a long-term winner and could be a takeover target for a larger food and beverage company. CNH Industrial (CNH) (2.4%; -16%) shares declined amid weakness in prices and demand for crops such as soybeans and corn that are likely to stall a recovery in demand for tractors and combines in 2026. Daimler Truck (DTRUY) (1.3%; -13%) reported a 61% decline in second quarter EPS as economic softness and tariff uncertainty negatively impacted demand for trucks, particularly in the U.S. Nevertheless, Daimler appears to be well positioned to weather current industry weakness and participate in the eventual recovery, given its strong financial position and market leading position in North America. Finally, Atlanta Braves Holdings (BATRA) (1.3%; -11%) shares declined during the quarter, giving back some of their strong returns over the last two years. Sports franchise values remain strong, and value is likely to eventually be surfaced in the stock through a sale of the team/company.

data.title: BlackRock Advantage Large Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 6.46% (Institutional shares) and 6.41% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the third quarter of 2025.
  • The fund's outperformance of its benchmark was largely driven by strong results from fundamental insights, with both traditional value and quality measures contributing meaningfully. Sentiment-driven signals also added to returns. Macro insights were more mixed, with some successfully capturing sector trends while others struggled amid shifting market themes.
  • From a sector-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. However, there were slight overweight allocations to the information technology (IT) and consumer discretionary sectors, and slight underweight exposures to the utilities and energy sectors.

Contributors

Fundamental insights were the most consistent drivers of performance. Value-focused insights, such as those evaluating companies with strong operating income relative to their stock price, contributed with an overweight exposure to the IT sector. Insights favoring firms investing heavily in research and development provided further gains. Within quality, signals that measure employee satisfaction and free-cash-flow generation contributed, especially in the health care and industrials sectors. Sentiment insights were helpful as signals tracking short-selling activity helped to maintain a successful overweight allocation to the communication services sector.

Detractors

Certain macro insights detracted during the quarter. Specifically, insights measuring industry-level invoicing trends were detrimental, particularly through positions in the consumer staples sector. Sentiment insights that track mobile app usage trends detracted through positioning in the energy sector. Within quality, an insight predicting sales growth using text-based company disclosures was unhelpful, especially in the financials sector.

Further insight

A cautious stance toward momentum styles that began in June caused weak performance to continue into July. The speculative tone around perceived artificial intelligence winners and subsequent deleveraging across the broader hedge fund community adversely impacted performance. This was characterized, at the time, by losses across informed investor, quality, and slower-moving measures of fundamental momentum. However, the same measures led returns in August and September. Against these dynamics, stock selection across the United States, particularly in the IT sector, struggled for traction. Notably, despite a continued pro-risk environment, gains in quality insights at quarter-end point to greater discrimination in investor positioning.
